welcomeMessage.en: Today's class takes us on an adventure! We are going to talk about travel preferences, packing habits, and how we like to get from place to place.
welcomeMessage.zh: 今天的课程将带我们去冒险！我们将谈论旅行偏好、打包习惯以及我们喜欢如何往返各地。

welcomeMessage.en: Get ready to share your dream destinations and travel stories!
welcomeMessage.zh: 准备好分享你梦想的旅行目的地和旅行故事！

questionsToThinkAbout.en: What are the pros and cons of traveling alone versus traveling with a large group of friends?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 独自旅行与和一大群朋友一起旅行的利弊是什么？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: If you had a totally flexible schedule and an unlimited budget, where would you go right now?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 如果你有完全灵活的时间表和无限的预算，你现在会去哪里？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: Have you ever had to compromise with a friend while on a trip? What happened?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 你在旅行中是否有过对朋友妥协的经历？发生了什么事？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: Do you prefer an active vacation (like climbing mountains) or a relaxing vacation (like a beach picnic)?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 你更喜欢活跃的假期（比如爬山）还是放松的假期（比如海滩野餐）？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: How do you handle unexpected problems when traveling?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 旅行中你如何处理突发问题？

vocabulary.definition: A large natural elevation of the earth's surface rising abruptly from the surrounding level.
vocabulary.example: Peter sees mountains every day, so he prefers traveling to the beach.

vocabulary.translation: 厌倦了... / 对...感到疲倦
vocabulary.example: I am tired of looking at the same scenery all the time.

vocabulary.definition: Located in the interior of a country / located near the sea.
vocabulary.example: Because my hometown is an inland city, I always want to visit coastal towns.

vocabulary.definition: A bag with shoulder straps that allow it to be carried on one's back.
vocabulary.example: I am a light traveler, so I only bring one small backpack.

vocabulary.definition: A greater liking for one alternative over another or others.
vocabulary.example: Do you have a preference between traveling by train or by plane?

vocabulary.definition: Able to be easily modified to respond to altered circumstances.
vocabulary.example: My vacation time is very flexible, so I can travel any day next week.

vocabulary.definition: Firmly fixed, not shaking or moving / moving evenly without jerks.
vocabulary.example: I like taking the high-speed train because the ride is incredibly steady and smooth.
Peter prefers trains over driving because he is afraid of car accidents.

vocabulary.definition: An estimate of income and expenditure for a set period of time.
vocabulary.example: If you want to travel for a month, you need to plan a careful budget.

vocabulary.definition: An outing or occasion that involves taking a packed meal to be eaten outdoors.
vocabulary.example: When we get to the mountain, let's have a picnic on the grass.

vocabulary.definition: The advantages and disadvantages of something.
vocabulary.example: Before booking the flight, let's discuss the pros and cons of this airline.

vocabulary.definition: An agreement reached by each side making concessions.
vocabulary.example: When traveling with a large group of friends, you often have to compromise on where to eat.

vocabulary.definition: A computer that is portable and suitable for use while traveling.
vocabulary.example: I was panicking at the airport because my expensive laptop was in my lost backpack!

sessionSummary: Today's class took us on an adventure! We had a fantastic conversation about travel preferences, packing habits, and how we like to get from place to place.

We started by comparing different destinations. Yating mentioned she loves visiting big cities. Kiki and Peter both chose the beach! Kiki explained that because her hometown is inland, she really loves coastal areas. Peter noted that since he lives around so many mountains, he is quite tired of seeing them.

When discussing packing, everyone agreed that traveling light is the best way to go. Instead of pulling heavy suitcases, Kiki and Peter prefer to just carry a backpack. We also talked about transportation; Kiki prefers flying because it is fast, while Peter favors the train because it is steady, smooth, and avoids the risk of car accidents.

We wrapped up the session with an airport baggage claim roleplay! Kiki did a great job explaining to the airport staff (Peter) that she had lost her black backpack, which importantly contained her laptop.

keyVocabulary.example: "Peter sees mountains every day, so he prefers traveling to the beach."

keyVocabulary.translation: 厌倦了... / 对...感到疲倦
keyVocabulary.example: "I am tired of looking at the same scenery all the time."
"Because my hometown is an inland city, I always want to visit coastal towns."
"I am a light traveler, so I only bring one small backpack."
"Do you have a preference between traveling by train or by plane?"
"My vacation time is very flexible, so I can travel any day next week."
"I like taking the high-speed train because the ride is incredibly steady and smooth."
"Peter prefers trains over driving because he is afraid of car accidents."
"If you want to travel for a month, you need to plan a careful budget."
"When we get to the mountain, let's have a picnic on the grass."
"Before booking the flight, let's discuss the pros and cons of this airline."
"When traveling with a large group of friends, you often have to compromise on where to eat."
"I was panicking at the airport because my expensive laptop was in my lost backpack!"

grammarTips.title: Using 'Tired of'
grammarTips.description: When you use the phrase 'tired of', it must be followed by a noun (a thing) OR an '-ing' verb (a gerund).
grammarTips.incorrect: "I am tired of see the mountains."
grammarTips.correct: "I am tired of SEEING the mountains. (Verb-ing) OR I am tired of the mountains. (Noun)"

grammarTips.title: Discussing Preferences: Prefer A to B
grammarTips.description: When comparing two things you like, use 'prefer [Noun/Verb-ing] to [Noun/Verb-ing]'. Do not use 'than'.
grammarTips.incorrect: "I prefer taking the train than taking a car."
grammarTips.correct: "I prefer taking the train TO taking a car. / I prefer the beach TO the city."

grammarTips.title: Pronunciation: Luggage vs. Baggage vs. Backpack
grammarTips.description: Remember that 'Luggage' and 'Baggage' mean the same thing (all of your bags together) and are uncountable (no 's'). 'Backpack' is just one specific type of bag and is countable.
grammarTips.correct: "I have three backpacks. OR I have a lot of luggage."

goals: Encourage full sentences when comparing modes of travel (e.g., 'I prefer taking the train to flying.').
Ensure students correctly use '-ing' verbs or nouns following phrases like 'tired of'.
Guide students to elaborate in roleplay scenarios by giving specific details, such as contents of a lost bag.

phases.title: Phase 1: General Travel Talk (15-20 Minutes)
phases.description: Warm-up with questions about preferences, budget, and styles of traveling.
questions: Do you prefer an active vacation or a relaxing vacation?
Are you someone who packs heavy luggage or just a small backpack? Why?

phases.title: Phase 2: Destinations and Compromise (20-30 Minutes)
phases.description: Examine inland vs coastal preferences and the challenges of group travel.
questions: What are the pros and cons of traveling alone versus with friends?
Have you ever had to compromise with a friend while on a trip? What happened?

phases.title: Phase 3: Airport Baggage Claim Roleplay (10-15 Minutes)
phases.description: Place students in a lost baggage scenario to practice descriptive language.
questions: Roleplay: One student lost their bag and must explain to the airport staff what it looks like and what's inside (e.g., a laptop).
